---
title: "Practice Cases"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
---

## Case Study 1: Rotavirus Vaccination Program

### Background

India evaluated the domestically-produced rotavirus vaccine (116E strain, ROTAVAC) for inclusion in the Universal Immunization Program. This case is based on Rose et al. (2017) [1].

### Study Details

- **Intervention**: Universal rotavirus vaccination using ROTAVAC
- **Population**: Birth cohort of Indian infants (~25 million annually)
- **Time horizon**: Lifetime
- **Perspective**: All-payer (government healthcare system)

### Base-Case Results

- **Incremental Cost**: ₹3,429 per DALY averted
- **Vaccine cost**: ~₹138 per infant (net of treatment savings)
- **Mortality reduction**: 34.6% reduction in rotavirus deaths (~34,000 lives saved annually)
- **Hospitalisation reduction**: 28.1%
- **WTP Threshold**: ₹88,500 per DALY (1× GDP per capita, 2014)
- **ICER**: ₹3,429 per DALY averted

### Sensitivity Analysis

- Worst-case scenario (vaccine efficacy at lower bound): ICER rises to ₹7,238 per DALY averted — still highly cost-effective
- Mortality reduction falls to 20.5% in the worst case
- Annual net programme cost: ~₹3.48 billion ($57 million)

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Solution & Interpretation

**Quadrant**: Northeast (NE) — More costly, more effective

**ICER Interpretation**: The rotavirus vaccination costs ₹3,429 per DALY averted, which is **highly cost-effective** — only 3.9% of India's GDP per capita threshold (₹88,500 at 2014 values).

**Decision**: Even in the most unfavourable scenario, the ICER (₹7,238/DALY) remains far below the threshold. This intervention has a **strong case for adoption**. India has indeed included ROTAVAC in the Universal Immunization Program.

**Key Points**:

- ICER is approximately 25 times lower than the threshold
- Domestic manufacturing (116E strain at ~$1/dose) dramatically reduces cost compared to imported alternatives ($15–20/dose)
- Vaccine efficacy of 53.6% against severe disease, confirmed by real-world effectiveness of 54% (Babji et al. 2025) [3]
- Narrow sensitivity range (₹3,429–₹7,238) indicates robust findings
:::

---

## Case Study 2: Pneumococcal Conjugate Vaccine (PCV)

### Background

HTAIn evaluated the introduction of 13-valent pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV13) into India's routine immunization schedule for reducing childhood pneumonia mortality. This case is based on Krishnamoorthy et al. (2019) [4].

### Study Details

- **Intervention**: PCV13 vaccination program (national level)
- **Population**: 10 birth cohorts, 2018–2027
- **Time horizon**: 10 years
- **Perspective**: Government healthcare system

### Base-Case Results

- **Incremental Cost**: ₹31,666 ($467) per DALY averted
- **WTP Threshold**: 1× GDP per capita of India
- **ICER**: ₹31,666 per DALY averted

### Probabilistic Sensitivity Analysis

- **Simulations**: Monte Carlo iterations
- **Mean ICER**: ₹44,008 ($649) per DALY averted
- **95% CI**: ₹25,380–₹78,732 per DALY averted
- **Most unfavourable scenario**: ₹1,57,520 ($2,323) per DALY averted — still cost-effective

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Solution & Interpretation

**Quadrant**: Northeast (NE) — More costly, more effective

**ICER Interpretation**: PCV13 vaccination costs ₹44,008 per DALY averted (PSA mean), which is **highly cost-effective** — well below India's WTP threshold.

**Decision**: Even in the worst-case scenario (₹1,57,520/DALY), the programme remains cost-effective. This intervention has a **strong case for implementation**. Several Indian states have introduced PCV into their immunization programs.

**Key Points**:

- Base-case ICER (₹31,666) is substantially lower than PSA mean (₹44,008), reflecting right-skewed uncertainty
- Wide confidence interval (₹25,380–₹78,732) reflects uncertainty in vaccine efficacy and coverage estimates
- Even the most unfavourable scenario yields an ICER that is cost-effective
- HTAIn has published a supporting outcome report recommending PCV introduction [5]
:::

---

## Case Study 3: Cy-Tb Test for Latent Tuberculosis Infection (LTBI)

### Background

Researchers evaluated the Cy-Tb diagnostic test for latent tuberculosis infection compared to the traditional Tuberculin Skin Test (TST), followed by preventive therapy in high-risk contacts. This case is based on Muniyandi et al. (2025) [6].

### Study Details

- **Intervention**: Cy-Tb test + 6-month isoniazid preventive therapy (6H TPT)
- **Comparator**: TST + 6-month isoniazid preventive therapy (6H TPT)
- **Population**: Hypothetical cohort of 100,000 TB contacts at high risk
- **Time horizon**: Decision-tree model
- **Perspective**: Health system

### Base-Case Results

- **Total cost (Cy-Tb arm)**: ~₹215 million
- **Total cost (TST arm)**: ~₹206 million
- **TB cases prevented**: 36,925 (Cy-Tb) vs 36,452 (TST) — difference of 473 additional cases prevented
- **WTP Threshold**: ₹2,16,590 per TB case prevented (1× GDP per capita, 2023)
- **ICER**: ₹18,658 per additional TB case prevented

### Sensitivity Analysis

- **PSA**: 55% of iterations in the NE quadrant of the CE plane
- **CEAC**: 55% probability of cost-effectiveness at the WTP threshold
- **Key drivers** (OWSA): Cy-Tb kit cost, Cy-Tb test negative rate, TST negative rate
- **Price threshold**: If Cy-Tb kit price drops from ₹376 to ₹286, the strategy becomes cost-saving (ICER = −₹353)

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Solution & Interpretation

**Quadrant**: Predominantly Northeast (NE) — More costly, more effective (55% of PSA iterations)

**ICER Interpretation**: The Cy-Tb test costs ₹18,658 per additional TB case prevented compared to TST. This is **cost-effective** at 8.6% of the WTP threshold (₹2,16,590).

**Decision**: The base-case ICER is clearly favourable, but PSA shows only 55% probability of cost-effectiveness — reflecting **moderate decision uncertainty**. This is a case where the point estimate looks good but the confidence around the decision is limited.

**Key Points**:

- Base-case ICER is well below threshold, but PSA probability (55%) is modest — contrast this with the near-certainty in the vaccine cases
- Kit cost is a major driver: price negotiation from ₹376 to ₹286 could shift from cost-effective to cost-saving
- This case illustrates why base-case ICER alone is insufficient — uncertainty analysis changes the recommendation strength
- **Policy recommendation**: Adopt with price negotiation; consider pilot implementation with real-world data collection
- Aligns with India's TB Elimination goals
:::

---

## Interactive Practice: Common Interpretation Errors

Test your understanding by identifying what's wrong with these interpretations:

### Scenario A

*An intervention has ICER = ₹3,50,000 per QALY gained. The researcher concludes: "This intervention is not cost-effective because it exceeds India's threshold."*

::: {.callout-warning collapse="true"}
## What's the Error?

**Error**: While the ICER (₹3,50,000) exceeds the typical threshold of ₹2,50,000, **cost-effectiveness is not binary**.

**Correct Interpretation**:

- The intervention exceeds the threshold by 40%
- It may still be acceptable depending on disease severity, budget impact, equity considerations
- Many HTA agencies accept interventions above threshold for life-threatening conditions
- Decision should consider: uncertainty, affordability, equity, priority populations
:::

### Scenario B

*A study reports 30% of PSA iterations in the Southeast quadrant. The researcher states: "30% of the time, this intervention dominates."*

::: {.callout-warning collapse="true"}
## What's the Error?

**Error**: PSA iterations don't represent "% of time" — they represent **parameter uncertainty**, not temporal variation.

**Correct Interpretation**:

- "Given parameter uncertainty, there is a 30% probability that this intervention is dominant (less costly and more effective)"
- This reflects uncertainty in model inputs (costs, effects, probabilities)
- Does NOT mean intervention will be dominant 30% of the time in practice
- Higher percentage in SE quadrant indicates stronger evidence for dominance
:::

### Scenario C

*An intervention falls in the Southwest quadrant (less costly, less effective) with ICER = ₹80,000 per QALY lost. The analyst concludes: "This should be rejected because it's less effective."*

::: {.callout-warning collapse="true"}
## What's the Error?

**Error**: SW quadrant interpretations are **inverted** — lower cost interventions may be preferred even if less effective.

**Correct Interpretation**:

- In SW quadrant, we ask: "Is the QALY loss acceptable given cost savings?"
- ₹80,000 per QALY **lost** means we save ₹80,000 for each QALY foregone
- Since ₹80,000 < ₹2,50,000 (the WTP threshold), the health loss costs more than the savings — **reject the cheaper option**
- If it were > ₹2,50,000 saved per QALY lost, the cheaper option could be preferred
- Decision should also consider: budget constraints, access, equity
:::

---

## Key Takeaways from Indian HTA Cases

### Successful Implementation Factors

1. **Strong evidence base**: Vaccination programs had robust RCT evidence
2. **Affordability**: Even cost-effective interventions need budget impact analysis
3. **Domestic production**: ROTAVAC's local manufacturing reduced costs significantly
4. **Equity considerations**: Universal immunization reaches underserved populations
5. **Policy alignment**: TB case aligns with national TB elimination goals

### Uncertainty & Implementation

- **Narrow sensitivity range** (ROTAVAC): High confidence in results — supports rapid adoption
- **Wide CI** (PCV): Additional real-world studies complement the economic evidence
- **Moderate uncertainty** (Cy-Tb): Base-case favourable but PSA probability modest — pilot programs and price negotiation recommended

---

## References

### Case 1: Rotavirus Vaccination (ROTAVAC)

1. Rose J, Homa L, Meropol SB, et al. Health impact and cost-effectiveness of a domestically-produced rotavirus vaccine in India: A model based analysis. *PLoS One*. 2017;12(11):e0187446. doi:[10.1371/journal.pone.0187446](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0187446)

2. Megiddo I, Colson AR, Nandi A, et al. Estimated impact and cost-effectiveness of rotavirus vaccination in India: Effects of geographic and economic disparities. *Vaccine*. 2014;32(Suppl 1):A151-A161. doi:[10.1016/j.vaccine.2014.07.070](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.vaccine.2014.07.070)

3. Babji S, Arumugam R, Gnanamurthy P, et al. Impact of the indigenous rotavirus vaccine Rotavac in the Universal Immunization Program in India during 2016–2020. *Nat Med*. 2025. doi:[10.1038/s41591-025-03998-9](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41591-025-03998-9)

### Case 2: Pneumococcal Conjugate Vaccine (PCV13)

4. Krishnamoorthy Y, Sakthivel M, Sarveswaran G, Eliyas SK. Impact and cost effectiveness of pneumococcal conjugate vaccine in India. *Vaccine*. 2019;37(4):623-630. doi:[10.1016/j.vaccine.2018.11.012](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.vaccine.2018.11.012)

5. Health Technology Assessment in India (HTAIn). Pneumococcal Conjugate Vaccine to prevent mortality and morbidity — Outcome Report. Department of Health Research, Government of India. Available at: [https://htain.dhr.gov.in](https://htain.dhr.gov.in/images/pdf/outcome_reports/66.Pneumococcal_Conjygate_Vaccine.pdf)

### Case 3: Cy-Tb Test for Latent Tuberculosis

6. Muniyandi M, Nagarajan K, Mathiyazhagan K, Tyagi K, Rajsekar K, Padmapriyadarsini C. Evaluating the cost-effectiveness of Cy-Tb for LTBI in India: a comprehensive economic modelling analysis. *Int Health*. 2025;17(3):259-269. doi:[10.1093/inthealth/ihae048](https://doi.org/10.1093/inthealth/ihae048)
